commentBody: Cool prize at the end!  Fun Wednesday puzzle.

The animation was fantastic!
But it still doesn't work on my phone - I get the Congratulations screen, but the x on the top right to return to the game doesn't work.  I can never see the finished puzzle.
Great crossword!

I'm good at RCs ... only in the sense that I can generally identify one when I see it.  (My birding skill is similar: "yes, that's a bird"). Solving, however... nope. Not a chance. 

This puzzle, thankfully, doesn't require as much twisting and swearing. (My autocomplete suggested "resignation", lol.)
What a theme. Could not have been executed more perfectly. Loved the theme clues, loved the revealer, loved the center cube, loved the animation. Kudos and well done

Speed cubers like to do the twist.

(They're a hit at the block party.)
A beauty! Not too easy, not full of hopeless trivia, with a theme I couldn’t guess - but then the light appeared!

I thought this Rubik’s Cube themed puzzle was ADORBS. Once I saw the theme, I wondered if there would be a color element, and I was pleased to be rewarded at puzzle’s end. Perhaps erroneously, and certainly immodestly, I consider myself a decent puzzler.  That said, I’ve never successfully completed a Rubik’s Cube, and stand in awe of those who can do them, especially those who can do them in less than a minute.

I loved the clue “Place for hot dates?” for OASES.

My heart really goes out to the poor nation of Haiti.

They've been plagued by bad government forever, fostering violence, poverty, misery.  

Then a huge earthquake hits that kills between 200,000 and 300,000 people.  Did you know that?  American news wasn't all that on top of it in 2010.  

Haiti is a  great example and a terrible warning for nations that can't dispose of their petty dictators.

There was a great New Yorker article about the history of Haiti I read many years ago.  It was devastating, and it's gotten worse since then.

A few get a break and get to come to this country, and are immediately accused of pet murder and ingestion, and are sent back to the rubble.

If karma is really a thing, then I greatly fear for our future.

Thank goodness understanding that inane cube thing wasn’t a requirement for solving the puzzle. Beat my Wednesday average by 3.5 minutes but, because “speedcuber” was filled by crosswords, I never saw that clue or noticed the answer. So after finishing and staring at those nine central squares for several minutes, I asked my wife if she could make any sense of it. Maybe it’s just me, but I wish I’d never seen this one.

What is the animation I’m supposed to be seeing?  After completing the puzzle I turned on overlays and it now shows me the central 9 squares in different colors, but there is no “animation” (iPhone with iOS 18.6.2)

Hey NYT: I looked up the puzzle's theme puzzle inventor and turns out his birthday is July 13. I suppose difficulty level of today's puzzle would have made it too challenging for a Monday crossword.
Also, I expected Tuesday's crossword to have something to do with Bastille Day but alas no.
Missed opportunities, if you ask me.
It turns out SPEED RACER was not the answer they were looking for . . .

I will have to show this puzzle to my older son in the morning, as he likes to attempt Rubik’s cubes. I have only ever succeeded in scrambling them worse than I started. I’m sure there is some logic to it that I am just not getting. For those of you who do know how, are there any tutorials online you’d recommend? (Preferably that don’t rely on watching a video; work and family keep me too busy for videos.)

Things I can do in the 2.76 seconds the Polish kid needed to solve the cube:

Select a knife
Select a potato
Speed cube the potato 
Go get the bandaids

Mind you, that’s  2.76 for each one separately.

Loved the animation at the end. And for those who may not have heard of it before, highly highly recommended this documentary called The Speed Cubers on Netflix- perfect mix of entertaining, interesting and heart warming- https://youtu.be/-wrTIWx_Z6k?is=uV6eXmyXHD-OFTAa

Good puzzle. Guessed the Rubik connection early enough, which I was very pleased about.

Tiny nit? Tony RYAN is the founder of Ryanair. I’ve never, ever heard it called anything polite except Ryanair (plenty of rude names for it, as it seems to go out of its way to be an extraordinarily churlish in dealing with its customers)

this one killed me. don't know actual time as clock continued running when I paused the solve, but I spent forever flyspecking. turns out the culprit was ARGoS and LOoIE.

I therefore deem it a worthy Wednesday, and shudder (with antici... pation) for Thursday.
Lifelong resident of the Great Lakes State, we do not use that acronym.
How fun was that?! Loved the animation at the end!!
cute
Xwordinfo.com version of solved puzzle. If you're not seeing the animation, you may see it here. https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/15/2026

I had no idea what a mortarboard was - I looked it up after the solve and was surprised to learn it's the academic cap I know quite well from my work at university. We call it "biret", apparently from the medieval Latin "birretum", small cap. I wear mine once per year, along with a toga, during the official part of the students' graduation (obviously we all take the costume off for the garden party/picnic that follows). It has no square board on it though - academic staff birety never do, unlike the student ones. Ours are just soft, square caps. I look utterly siIIy in mine, like an old-timey Catholic priest - I always take a selfie and send it to my mother-in-law so she can have a laugh 🤣 2024 graduation at my faculty: https://imgur.com/a/luai9qd

I guess animation works for most. As I don’t see any complaints. 

It doesn’t work for me. But again. Not necessary. I get the idea. 

Finger painting = NAIL.ART.  super fun. 

Is this puzzle a record for number of first banners in one grid?
RYAN, LAURA, LOUIS, RUE (first name?), TESSA. 
I’ll exclude ODIN.
Nice one! I didn't guess the theme until the end

I was reading about Spain's World Cup victory over France and came across this sentence: "By the end of the game, Spain were playing keep-ball, toying with their opponents as ‘Oles’ reverberated around the stadium." So I searched for a video and found an Instagram clip from USA Today Sports of fans celebrating after the match. https://www.instagram.com/reel/DaylAeKxI6U/ I got a kick (!) out of seeing that little bit of crosswordese come to life.
Best Sam Corbin column yet, and that’s saying something. Today I have a new emotion: “I wish I felt otherwise!” Thanks, Sam 🙂

How do we feel about this one?
Each square revealing itself till the puzzle’s done. 
Some solve with ease, some will struggle
Tossing aside while cursing its maker
I tend to believe there be many crucible solvers
Who are still stumped by Erno
And his not so simple Rubik’s cube
Love the theme! A little easy for a Wednesday, but very enjoyable.

Some feedback from wine country, it’s a common misconception that many rosé wines are blends. The pink color is from an abbreviated period of leaving the skin on the grape during fermentation, not mixing red and white wine.
Never heard of it being referred to as RYAN. Many names, few pleasant but never RYAN.

Super fun - love the theme and the fact that Erno’s name is actually mixed up as the cube in the center :) 

That being said, I struggled a bit with the ATRA/ARGUS/LOUIE crossing, but overall amazing puzzle!
For me the crossing of 6 across and  and 7 down was naticky. I hadn't heard of either brand though at least "hot TOPIC" meanings something in English which is more than can be said for "ATRA".
Hands up all who had ARGoS crossing LOoIE...
Tricky, tricky, tricky for a Wednsday puzzle but really fun. Got stuck for a bit on 48 Down when I entered SCORE rather than OPERA, but recovered when I realized 45 Across was FLYING COLORS.
Another crossword, another animation that fails to work:(
Fast and quirky. Fun puzzle!

This was a fun puzzle.  Fortunately, when I heard that the lyrics to "Louie, Louie" were dirty, the name and spelling of the song became permanently embedded in my 13-year-old head.

So I missed the hardest cross, which we know defines the difficulty of a puzzle, right?  😉

I am currently refraining from going outside, because the greater Grand Marais metropolitan area has the worst air in the country at the moment.  It's climbing down (so far) from a peak almost doubling the floor for the worst Air Quality Index of "Hazardous".

Super hot and dry conditions are fueling wild fires near the Boundary Waters area.  Sensors in the Boundary Waters registered 100 F.

Let me repeat that:  Temperatures got to at least 100 F in the Boundary Waters.  And no, Mr. Clever Pants, this was not in the midst of a fire.

There's a strange sickly yellow-orange hue to the sky.  It feels quite apocalyptic.
I've heard Ryanair being referred to in a lot of ways but never as Ryan
Read the theme as “TURNING SCRAMBLES COLORS.” Which it does.

I was never going to guess the theme today. TURNING PRO MAD SCRAMBLES FLYING COLORS Something sports related? Aerobatics? Loved the scrambled ERNO RUBIK. Everybody knows Louie Louie, so I'll go with The Argus https://youtu.be/JmzGMhE_ELs?si=eYBRYp-UqvRiHIpy

Question: Can you solve a Rubik's cube?”
Answer: "ER, NO." 

🧩 (Loved the SPEED CUBER theme today!)

A lovely Wednesday that I confess I found easier than Tuesday this week. I hadn’t come across the term SPEEDCUBER before, but it’s self explanatory so helped me unSCRAMBLE the name.

RYAN. Never called Ryanair that. Many other names, none complimentary, but not that. Not a big fan of Mr O’Leary, but he’s a clever so and so.

I SAY. Yeah, no. Maybe in Eton. Yes it’s a not uncommon parody of a British Toff, so a perfectly cromulent clue. I’m afraid my immediate response was far more Anglo Saxon.

I wanted to pop cider into the Hazy clue as that’s one of our best sellers in the Hall bar. A strong, cloudy, locally brewed witches potion. 

Now then. The flags are out, the bunting is up, the bar is stocked. We’re ready for kick off against those South Americans Who Shall Not Be Named. We’re still looking for justice against the ‘hand of god’ of 1986. If you don’t hear from me tomorrow I’ll be in mourning. Or hungover. Or both.

Tough for a Wednesday, and for some reason I wasn't seeing the first several answers, which weren't that hard, so this pushed me almost to my average time.

Finished the whole thing and still didn't really get it until I deciphered the awesome animation.

Thanks!
Is there supposed to be a picture or animation? I'm getting a blur.
Ok, that was awesome.

I got the theme after the second themer, and noticing the nine circles (but FLYING COLORS was my favorite) . . . "Ooh, let ther be animation, let there be animation!" Maria Muldaur singing about warm fruit: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5x_IwuXzbKM&list=RD5x_IwuXzbKM&start_radio=1 (There were several YouTubes of this song, live--all featured a tambourine and a yellow rose. I chose this one because its graphics uses Peignot, the official font of the 70's.)

Emus ate my first,
so I apologize if this ends up being a dupe, 
but in short-

I love animation like a 
kid on Saturday morning in the 70’s.

One of my boys is a speed cuber.

So,
two thumbs up.
It was on a RYAN flight just last week that a passenger was almost s*cked out of the plane when the window next to him broke.  (It was a Boeing 737, so perhaps blame will be apportioned.)

ARGUS or ARGoS?

The Greek is spelled with an omicron, so I put in ARGoS first.  LOoIE can be spelled with a double-o, but I thought that would be unlikely here, so I switched to the Latin spelling ARGUS.

It's a little bit funny.  Part of me wants to complain that if we're referencing a Greek deity, we should transliterate "properly".
But if someone refers to Athene or Akhilleus (not to mention Platon or Aristoteles), it just looks wrong.

Which just goes to show....
Something, I guess. I'm not sure what.

2.76 seconds????
Seriously???

It takes me longer than that to think, "Oh, look!  A Rubik's cube."

This is how you do a tribute puzzle about Erno and his creation.

Normally tribute puzzles present random facts about the subject, but look at what this puzzle does.  Sweet wordplay theme answers – I adore the double meanings of TURNING PRO and FLYING COLORS -- and an animation that visually represents the subject morphing from scrambled to unscrambled AND consists of the inventor’s name.  Wow!

I loved the lively longs – MAD SCRAMBLES, PEDAL POWER, POETRY SLAM, SPEED CUBER, and TURNING PRO.  The grid has six longs, these five, which are NYT crossword debuts, and FLYING COLORS, which is a once-before.  Again, wow!

Oh, I’ve seen those videos of people solving a cube in under five seconds, and every time my jaw drops and I think, “Man, we humans are one heck of a species!”

After seeing how artfully and creatively, J&J, you two tributed the Rubik’s cube, I found myself once again thinking the same thing. Thank you for this!
Fun puzzle. Loved the pretty Rubik cube graphic. Liked the clueing for 'All in'.

Even with the highlighted central squares I don't know how anyone could have guessed the theme before getting the revealer. A kudo or two if you did!

Still fun.

And I love the column photo of the guy who's completely flummoxed as he tries to spell out YMCA to an empty arena.
As a speedcuber myself, it took me *way* too long to catch on. Fun puzzle!
This was fun. Never had what it takes to be a “cuber” of any type. Just not in my wheel house. Did enjoy the revealer at the end. Thanks! Y’all have a great Wednesday!

This felt challenging for a Wednesday, though I finished around 20 percent under my average time…that it seemed challenging is not a complaint, as I appreciated it (though it did bring up issues of inferiority, as someone who has never solved a Rubik’s Cube…not even in 2.76 days, not to mention seconds). So, thanks to the constructors for a very nice start to my day.
Came here to comment on the Mini. Please stop calling “jumbo shrimp” an oxymoron. It’s not. Shrimp is the name of the animal, obviously, not a reference to size. Words can have two meanings.

I came here to nominate this as Worst Puzzle of the Year. But first I read all the comments and learned that I'm a minority of two. Oh, I solved the puzzle, filled in the theme answers and the reveal with the help of the boring fill. Never heard of SPEEDCUBER, so I had no idea what that was or how those theme answers related to the reveal. I'm good at anagrams, but was still thinking it was sports-related, the usual kind, not like twisting a Rubik's cube or downing hot dogs. And my dead tree edition doesn't do any animations.

On the rare occasions that I don't get a theme, I read Wordplay and all the comments and then feel enlightened. This is not one of those times. My mileage did indeed vary.
SPEEDCUBER/UAE was my very last fill, so the animation was a total and charming surprise.

A perfectly fine Wednesday from Jonathan and Jeff even if I could care less about Rubik Cubes. And how come no one has mentioned that once the squares rearrange themselves the Across entries are no longer words? Where are the nit pickers this morning? A few posters have mentioned The Kingsmen song in reference to 21 Across. My brain went to Brother Louie by Story, cover of a Hot Chocolate song*. https://youtu.be/d1WYhN6zdxI?si=uHmAXpudFwosYKzV *A Hot Chocolate song, You S*xy Thing, is also featured in the movie The Full Monty.

SPEED CUBER ??
Jeez, that sounds like something NSFW.
Wasn't hard to figure out but the phrase falls oddly on the ear.

I never got into the Rubik thing, but the themers were delightfully twisty and apt. Good puzzle.
Two things most of my friends and colleagues know about me is that I am a daily NYT xword solver/addict and my son is a world-ranked speedcuber. I may have to turn my phone off today.

Well... tough one for me. Long workout and must confess that I wasn't quite grasping the trick even when I was done. Had to come here to get it. No big deal - that's just me. And... puzzle find today. Don't recall another one quite like this. A Sunday from May 27, 2001 by Dana Motley with the title: "A Matter of Degree." Theme was a string of answers, each clued as "Start of a quote," "Quote, part 2," etc., etc. Here those are: CANYOUIMAGINE DRFAHRENHEITANDDR CELSIUS TALKINGDRFAHRENHEIT WOULDSAYMANITSHOTIN HEREAND DRCELSIUSWOULDSAY ITSNOTTHATHOT Whew (and wow). Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/27/2001&h=21a ....

Wonderful puzzle with a great Wednesday “twist”. Much better choice than a rebus with MPH.

I too had ARGOS first but on reflection that spelling seems to be reserved for Odysseus’s dog (although the Greek spelling is the same), one of the most poignant scenes in Homer which I won’t spoil for those about to see The Odyssey (I can’t imagine it not being included, and I think I saw a snippet in the preview). The other is Hector holding up his infant son on the walls of Troy in The Iliad. These are the personal moments that show the true genius of the Homeric epic and why they resonate with us over two and a half centuries later.

Good morning! Nice Weds. and liked the animation. I figure most regular xword solvers knew the anagram, as "Erno" seems to appear frequently. I solved the cube once, and that was good enough for me. Kudos to the mind that is a SPEEDCUBER.
Love the photo that heads the column. I admit to enjoying reels of ballet dancers in rehearsal. Incredible athletes & artists.

Nice ODE to the Rubik’s Cube! I remember getting one for Christmas the year they came out. I was never able to solve it completely. I could only do two sides. Fast forward ~25 years…my oldest son got one for his birthday and had it mastered within the week 🤯 He was one of those speed solvers that blew my mind. I also liked the cool animation. Thanks, Jonathan and Jeff!
Not an enjoyable puzzle. One that sacrifices enjoyment to fit a poor revealer with milquetoast themed answers. Constructor trying to be too clever and forgot to inject the fun. Pass.

My heavens,
am I ever a sucker 
for a wee bit of animation
with the solve.

What am I, five?

Don’t care, it makes me instantly feel all whimsy-like.

And one of my boys is a speed cuber.

Ergo, 👍👍

This puzzle clicked into place for me.  

I’ve completed 6k+ puzzles on the NYT app over the years (time well spent if you ask me) and this was my fastest Wednesday ever.  Guess I was in the zone.

I’m not the fastest crossworder you’ll ever meet but I’m pleasantly surprised that it happened  with a puzzle theme of speedy solving!

This made me break out into a huge grin! I loved the theme. The animation at the end was cute.

On a personal note, I'm currently stuck inside a cabin on my last day of vacation due to an enormous smoke cloud blowing in from the Superior National Forest, where there are 17 wildfires burning. Its heartbreaking. I have a simple request: be more mindful of how you are polluting this earth. Drive less, buy less, use less water. Really think about what you do that might be making these fires worse.

RUBIC's Pointless Toy is the star of the show today.  I had to suss out his first name, and that of the  NBA player/coach, not to mention Liam Neeson's trilogy...  
Best "Fell into the Trap" move on my part was coming up with NAIF ART for "finger-painting"... after which I had to say, "Aw, FOUIE."  The guys in the Marching Band were fond of bellowing the "LOUIE, LOUIE" lyrics--just another reason to be glad when football season was over!

See you later, unless you get sucked out the window on a RYAN-Air flight....

I have never done The Cube, but found this puzzle pretty easy. Had ARGOS / LOOIE until the end tho ;)
Went to Chrome version to see the animation. Now back to reading hard news :(

Wow is that a wonderful puzzle.  I had no idea what a SPEEDCUBER could be, but knowing that the cross would be BORSCHT, I filled in the "C" and hoped for the best.  As a patent attorney, I was mildly embarrassed that I couldn't unscramble the inventor jumble at the center, but sometimes it's nice to be surprised too.

I am so sad!  No animation - and the solved puzzle is covered by a blue page with something that looks like a cloud and a hill.

Going to re-start my computer and see if that fixes the glitch...
Truly wonderful construction!
Just adding another voice in support of the puzzle and the fun theme! I think there will always be naysayers but today they seem particularly unjustified.
Every time I attempt ruthless downsizing up pops a small piece of white cardboard, the Rubik’s cube cheat sheet which helped me solve it in about the same time today’s puzzle took.

Some folks, me included, did not get a sharp image of the animation.  On my screen, it looks like a grey outline of the state of Utah that contains a light blue sky, a cloud, and a green mound. The lower right corner has a diagonal line separating the corner from the rest of the puzzle.   Would someone be so kind as to describe what it should be and how the image relates to the puzzle?  I know it should have something to do with the Rubki's cube.

I enjoyed the theme, was ambivalent about the graphics, and lamented the cluing, which seemed too straight-forward for a Wednesday.

Did enjoy reading a bit about the development of the Empire apple by pomologists at Cornell University. The original seed for the variety, a cross between Macintosh and Red Delicious, was sown in 1945. In 1966, Cornell commercially released the Empire with no restrictions or brand management. This certainly was generous; for comparison, the Univ. of Minn. has made more than $16 million from royalties for its development of Honeycrisp.

FLYING COLORS indeed, as I threw the maddening device across the room. I did manage to solve it eventually, with the cheat sheet, and never fiddled with it again. My nephew is a SPEED CUBER, which blows my mind. Amusing little puzzle, and I was happy to solve the word jumble cube before triggering the animation.
What a fun puzzle! And I loved the animation, too!
Today's New Yorker puzzle is a "beginner friendly" Robyn Weintraub puzzle. Not as sparkly or difficult as one of her Fridays here, there's some nice stuff particularly some of the Downs, I thought.
I had to come to the column to get the theme. I print the puzzle and solve it on paper, so there were no colors or animation for me. Oh, well.

Like many others, all I saw was a blue square with a ? Kinda feel cheated, but I enjoyed the game anyway.

My cleaning crew was late today, so had to cancel/change plans for the day, so I am going to head over to the hospital where I volunteer, and walk in their air conditioning, have lunch there, and drop some stuff off for the food bank we have for employees. Isn't it sad that we pay health care workers so little that we need a food bank for them?

Have a safe day, people.

poem made from words in today’s puzzle

a/ maybe the moon’s bluffing
flying in its path
a rock, a thing
it rose a million million 
d/ days
to rest there
like a finger 
a/ alas

I love puzzles, but for some reason never warmed to the cube. I know that the six middle squares are fixed, I know there's logic and method involved, but I never was moved to learn it. I'm amazed watching SPEEDCUBERs but the thing that really freaks me out is blindfolded speedcubers; here's video of a kid setting a new world record of 12.1 seconds. https://www.youtube.com/shorts/OcYQXI72NMI?feature=share
Please tell me how to contact the NY Times crossword puzzle staff.  Thank you

I also got the huge Utah shaped blur for the animation feature.  
When I refreshed the page I saw the wee moving graphic.  
I enjoyed it so much more before I saw it.
I may be a cubist rube.
I have never heard of a "speed cuber" (is it two words?) and I never figured out what the blinking letters in the middle were all about. Hopefully, I never will.

In the past I would go to look at the Wordplay column and it would open in the NY Times app. Now it opens in the Games app. I miss the old way, it was easier to flip back and forth when it came time to cheat a little. I mean c’mon, I’m already frustrated enough when I can’t solve without help!
Maybe my favorite crossword of all time! Such a clever theme, well-constructed, and the animation at the end was an unexpected delight. Bravo!

I didn't find this one too difficult, a nice surprise. And I got the animation. 
I was stumped by the apple for ages, trying different states, because I forgot about the Empire State, despite the quite large building named after it. And I didn't know Tracy and Lauren could refer to a man.
Nice.

Absolutely terrible crossword today.

Oases for hot dates? No, just no
Contents of some carriers? (could very literally be anything)
Expecting anybody to know what a liberetto is? Pretentious 
Poetry slam instead of slam poetry? Abysmal

Listen, I’m not blaming the people who make them, but I do long for the days when Rubik‘s Cubes were more challenging. Not sure if it’s the way they’re scrambled, or fine-tuning of the inner workings, or the colour-editors making the blues more vibrant, but the solve times just keep going down. I’m sure it’s just to sell more cubes to people with shorter attention spans caused by TikTok. And don’t suggest it’s just that I’ve been doing them for a few years -  because I still can’t solve one. (Still, even I can see that this was more like a Tuesday cube.)

Nice to see this tight Wednesday, a tribute but hardly arcane for non-cubers like me, from a newer constructor and an old hand (POW!). Solved on my stone tablet clockwise from the NW, guessing what would be in the circles after seeing the revealer, but finishing the clock face before filling in the jumble and then unscrambling the letters in my head. I did the puzzle shortly after it dropped last night, but didn't post, reasonably sure the early comments, if not all of them, would be dominated by the success or failure of the animation.

Fun puzzle and eye-catching solution! 
I thought the intersection of HOMES and rapper was a Natick, until I remembered what HOMES stood for...
Turns out I've been to Natick. I had forgotten. I was visiting friends around Framingham and when I got sick they took me to urgent care in nearby Natick.
cute animation at end. I got "SPEEDCUBER" early on and was befuddled -- couldn't connect the dots. But overall a fun puzzle when I figured it out.

Very cute puzzle! Loved the graphic at the end. I would not have come up with that competitor, but all became clear when I figured out the sacred bird of ancient Egypt (Isis? no, that's a goddess...Iris? no, a flower...) I-something-I-something, and suddenly I remembered the beautiful IBIS. Fun solve today! Thanks, Jonathan Raksin and Jeff Chen!

Unusual for me, I got all three of the theme answers before getting the revealer at all. I was going through it quickly, but that area around the revealer was my slowest, for whatever reason. 

Well, part of that reason was because, again for whatever reason, I was bopping around the grid, which I don't usually do. And it turns out I missed seeing a few clues that were actually gimmes. And they were surrounded with a few names I didn't know, so that was a bit silly of me. However, it was kind of fun to bop around instead of my usual method... kind of the same way that I now like to change my wordle starting word everyday.  Such a wild risk-taker, aren't I!? Born to be wild!!

So I focused on trying to make sense of the three theme answers and finally got the aha that it might be about the Rubik's cube, and verified it by correctly unscrambling the middle box, which I haven't bothered with at first. 

Anyhow, I thought it was a fun solve!  I enjoyed it!  Well, other than inclusion of the dreaded APED. It, and cur, clued as they typically are, are just so distasteful for me. Poor pups and apes. It must feel really bad for them when they do the NYT crossword and see themselves mocked. 😏
Woof.....that one didn't come together until the very end for me!

Well I logged in on the website to see the animation and wow it was so worth it! I had one mistake and checked puzzle. The name of the movie I had one letter wrong: TAKuN for TAKEN. Wow loved that groovy trick!

Like others said, that one just felt off. The theme entries felt off. Maybe in the competitive cubing world things like MAD SCRAMBLE, FLYING COLORS and TURNING PRO make sense but not to me.. To complicate it further how many people know Rubik’s cube competitors are call SPEED CUBERS? 

Rating:
4/10
What a mess of a puzzle
OMG! 40A I kept thinking Leslie Nielsen and wrote naked for the Naked Gun Franchise! I couldn't let it go and finally went to auto check.  Anyway, both were great trilogies.

Okay - strangest puzzle I've ever encountered. A Sunday from July 2, 1989 by Ernst Theimer with the title "Fractured Polysyllables" Some theme clues and answers: "OPPOSED TO A BANNER SHARE" CONFLAGRATION - so... CON FLAG RATION "BEEF ABOUT 3 A.M. RECORD PLAYING." DISCONSOLATE - so.... DISC ON SO LATE "PANNING THE COLD CUTS SHOP" DELIBERATING - so... DELI BERATING "HOW TO MAKE MEN TAT" ENTERTAINMENT so.... ENTER TA IN MENT "CONTRACT DEBT CHITS" INCURIOUS so... INCUR IOUS "SVELTE MONARCH" - THINKING so... THIN KING And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=7/2/1989&h=48d .....

Speaking of 40A [Liam Neeson action trilogy] TAKEN--I saw the first of the trilogy years ago. It was quite amazing. Liam Neeson's character is like a superpower/superhero. He has some amazing conquests.

The movie has an unbelievable scene of an auction in it. Has anybody seen it? Do things like that actually happen? It seemed to have a certain realism.

I saw the second installment as well. Neeson's superpowers become even more unbelievable.

Upon finishing the puzzle I see there's a graphic "reward", but I think it's broken for me - looks like a giant "document icon". I'm accessing the site on a MacBook with Chrome. What's the real picture look like?

@Francis
A side note about Haiti's poverty. When France agreed to independence for Haiti in 1804 after revolution, France later in 1825, using gunships as a threat, demanded reparations from Haiti for lost income, including lost slave income. Haiti agreed, literally with a gun to its head, and paid France yearly from 1825 until 1947, including payments to other debtors, mostly American, who helped finance Haiti's payments. In all, it's estimated that this "independence debt" cost Haiti around $560 million in 2022 dollars: the main reason for Haiti's poverty.

@Francis FWIW, I’ve read that karmic debt is incurred only through volitional action, such that simply being born American would not create karmic effects. However: woe to those setting our policy. @Rachel when I was young I learned how to solve one face of it, which isn't all that hard once you know how. I satisfied myself with that.

@Rachel

My mathematically inclined childhood friend was great with a Rubik's cube. I don't think I've ever managed to solve it even once 🤣

@Rachel The how to solve it book I had back in the eighties suggested starting with a solved cube and seeing what various combinations of moves do to the pattern. There are a huge number of combinations. (8 factorial times 12 factorial times 2 to the twelfth times 3 to the eighth). Though some proportion ( eleven in twelve?) cannot be reached without dismantling and reassembling the cube. You basically need to be able to solve one face, then no the combination of moves to get each of the four edge pieces next to that face into position without changing the face and then know the combination of moves to move the pieces around on the face opposite the solved face without changing anything else. (And solving the first face involves have each of the corner and edge pieces in the right place not just in a random order with the right color showing on one side)

Thanks for sharing your photo.  You're right!  Those "birety" look quite clerical!  It makes one wonder where are strange traditions come from (and makes me realize that we don't even recognize that our traditions are strange until we see that others do it differently).

I couple of notes on American usage (for future reference):  Americans don't call graduation attire "togas", but "robes".  Judges, too, where robes professionally.

And we Americans use "faculty" to refer to the academic staff;  a professor is a member of the faculty.
What you are calling "faculty", we call a "department" (for example the Biology Department in the College of Arts and Sciences) or "school" (e.g. the School of Law at the University of Michigan).

"are"  ---> "our"

For me, those two words sound similar, but you'd think I'd know that they're spelled differently.

@The X-Phile

I’d say that the “togas” are more often called gowns, not robes, in the US, although this may be more for the graduates than for the faculty.

@Marie

We all look like !d!ots in those s7up!d things, and we're sweating like pigs under the togas - graduation takes place in June/July, and A/C can't handle cooling down that huge room when hundreds of people are in there.

@The X-Phile

Thanks for that. However, my choice of words works better than you think in the context of my post. Our wydziały are always translated as faculties into English, no exceptions. Departments are a tier below that. We almost never use the term "school" when describing a university, as schools are what comes before uni.

We literally call those robes togas, even though they don't drape like a toga 🤷🏽

And don't make me start on how to translate our degrees and titles into English - there basically is no way to do that since our system is nothing like yours.

@LlRand

I think you are being a bit pedantic. If you look down any common list of oxymorons you will see that one of the words is being used in a slightly different manner than literal to make it work. “Awfully good” is an example, obviously “awfully” doesn’t mean “bad” it means “very much”. Obviously shrimps are creatures which can be relatively larger or smaller than each other and other things, and this example is a bit tongue in cheek. But I argue that an oxymoron can be two words that literally don’t seem to go together but figuratively or metaphorically make some sense.
